topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The printout lay crumpled on the kitchen counter, the ink blurring under my thumb. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to steady myself. Each line of the genetic report felt like a physical blow, a cascade of unfamiliar names and percentages that rearranged the foundations of my entire existence. I stared at the screen, my breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. Who were these people? The silence in the apartment was suddenly deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse in my ears. I wanted to scream, to smash the paper into a million pieces, but my throat was tight, refusing to cooperate. I kept running my eyes over the data, searching for an anchor, a point of certainty in this swirling vortex of disbelief and sudden, profound disorientation.

[story 2]

I reread the final section three times, my eyes burning from the screen light. A cold sweat slicked my palms, making the paper slippery. Every time I glanced up, I felt a tightening in my chest, a pressure building behind my ribs that made deep breaths feel like an impossible task. The air in the room seemed too thick, suffocating. I paced the length of the living room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, unable to settle my gaze on the evidence anymore. Whispering the names aloud felt like a betrayal of the gravity of the information. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles ache, and I kept rubbing my temples, trying to physically push the confusing reality out of my head.

[story 3]

The computer screen glowed harshly in the dim room, illuminating the stark, undeniable facts. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric of the seat digging into my spine, and let out a slow, ragged exhale. It wasn't a gasp; it was more like air leaking slowly from a punctured tire. My gaze drifted from the DNA markers to the framed photograph on the shelf—a picture of a smiling family I had always believed was mine. A tremor ran through my hands, causing me to instinctively pull them away from the keyboard. The world felt tilted, as if the floor beneath me had dissolved. I stood up abruptly, needing distance, needing to move, anything to escape the overwhelming sense of unraveling.
